methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Cerebras is a publicly traded semiconductor company founded by Andrew Feldman and co-founders, currently valued at $39.2B after raising $6.6B+. The broader Chips & Semiconductors sector comprises 9 total peers tracked—3 public (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private entities including Ouster, Aeva Technologies, SambaNova Systems, PsiQuantum, and Etched.

Standard peer-median valuation methodology is not applicable here. While 9 same-sector comparables exist, the narrow peer sets—same-stage, adjacent-stage, and similar-valuation-tier filters—each yielded fewer than 3 datapoints, the minimum threshold for robust triangulation. Filtering by stage or valuation clustering produces insufficient observations to support a median-based range. A broad all-sector median would introduce material noise given Cerebras's distinct technology positioning and scale relative to most private peers.

The current $39.2B valuation reflects the company's public market pricing and cannot be independently cross-checked through conventional multiples-based triangulation given data constraints. Key uncertainties include the appropriate peer cohort for Cerebras—whether comparables should weight the three large-cap public names (NVDA, AMD), the single specialized peer (ALAB), or the fragmented private frontier-chip set. Further clarity on capital deployment efficiency and revenue trajectory would strengthen confidence in the valuation. Without sufficient narrowly-filtered comparables, analyst judgment on stage-specific risk premia becomes critical.
